parsed.summary: The v0.4.7 commit renames the npm scope and modifies the setup command flow, but the diff is not accessible for detailed line-level review. Based on the commit message and changed files, the primary risks are: (1) scope rename is a breaking change for existing users referencing the old package name, (2) the setup command modification adds an interactive menu when already configured, which could break non-interactive/CI workflows, and (3) release_card.md has significant rewrites that may contain incorrect setup instructions. These need verification against the actual diff.

findings.title: Scope rename may break existing installations and references
findings.body: The commit message explicitly mentions renaming the npm scope. Package renames are breaking changes: existing users with the old package installed will not receive updates, any lockfiles or scripts referencing the old scope will break, and there may be no redirect/deprecation notice on the old package. Without a deprecation strategy or migration path documented, existing users will silently stop getting updates.
findings.recommendation: Verify the diff adds a deprecation notice on the old package name, or document the migration path in CHANGELOG.md and release_card.md. If no migration tooling exists, add `npm deprecate` on the old scope.

findings.title: Interactive menu in setup command may hang in non-interactive contexts
findings.body: The commit adds an interactive menu to the /glm:setup command when already configured. If this command is invoked in non-interactive environments (CI, scripts, piping), the interactive prompt will hang indefinitely waiting for user input, with no timeout. This is a common cause of CI pipeline stalls.
findings.recommendation: Verify the diff includes a check for TTY/non-interactive mode, or a flag like --force/--non-interactive to skip the menu. Without it, any scripted invocation of setup will deadlock.

findings.title: CHANGELOG and release_card rewrite may have stale or incorrect scope references
findings.body: release_card.md saw 273 additions and 103 deletions — a near-complete rewrite. If old npm scope references remain in any example commands, installation snippets, or badge URLs, users will follow broken instructions. Mixed old/new scope references in documentation are a common regression during renames.
findings.recommendation: Grep the full diff for any remaining references to the old scope name. Ensure all install commands, import statements, and URLs consistently use the new scope.

next_steps: Inspect the actual diff with `git diff 7766943^..7766943` to verify the interactive menu has a non-interactive escape hatch.
Search the diff for the old scope name to confirm no stale references remain in documentation or configuration.
Verify whether the old npm package has been deprecated or if a migration utility exists for existing users.
Test the setup command in a non-TTY environment to confirm it does not hang.
